metadata.title: Clifti-GPT benchmark AnnData: reference and query splits for federated single-cell experiments
metadata.description:
Clifti-GPT benchmark AnnData

Prepared reference and query AnnData (.h5ad) files used to reproduce Clifti-GPT experiments across six public single-cell cohorts. Each archive contains the two files required by the experiment registry in the Clifti-GPT repository. Gene expression is filtered to the scGPT vocabulary; highly variable genes (HVG) are not used for subsetting.

Related deposit (init weights, separate record): 10.5281/zenodo.20489646

This deposit provides the minimal benchmark bundle for reproducing Clifti-GPT fine-tuning, annotation, and embedding experiments. Files are split into reference (labeled training) and query (held-out test) AnnData objects per cohort, matching the layout expected under data/scgpt/benchmark/ in the Clifti-GPT codebase.

File

Cohort

Contents inside zip

ms.zip

Multiple Sclerosis

ms/reference_annot.h5ad, ms/query_annot.h5ad

hp5.zip

Human Pancreas

hp5/reference.h5ad, hp5/query.h5ad

lung.zip

Lung-Kim

lung/reference_annot.h5ad, lung/query_annot.h5ad

cl.zip

Cell line

cl/reference.h5ad, cl/query.h5ad

covid.zip

COVID-19 (uncorrected)

covid/reference-raw.h5ad, covid/query-raw.h5ad

myeloid.zip

Myeloid (base)

myeloid/reference_adata.h5ad, myeloid/query_adata.h5ad

metadata.title: KAM Stability Certificates for Circadian Rhythms and H³ Geodesic Phylogenetic Distance Metrics
metadata.description: Biological oscillators are near-integrable Hamiltonians governed by KAM theory. Theorem OG-BIO01: Circadian oscillator passing KAMCheck() maintains amplitude stability under perturbations ε<1/21≈4.76% — matching jet-lag recovery bounds. H³ geodesic distance computes evolutionary divergence with lower distortion than Euclidean embedding. ZPL applied to gene regulatory networks: GRN passing ZPL has zero net transcriptional bias — formal homeostasis certificate. Applications: drug chronotherapy, cancer cell cycle disruption, CRISPR off-target minimization. κ=1.60225455 from ORCID 0009-0003-2911-444X. ZKP commitment block 40840.

data.title: KAM Stability Certificates for Circadian Rhythms and H³ Geodesic Phylogenetic Distance Metrics

metadata.description:
The files included in this Zenodo record support “Epistatic impacts of cis- and trans-regulatory mutations on the distribution of mutational effects for gene expression in Saccharomyces cerevisiae” by Eden McQueen, Bing Yang, and Patricia J. Wittkopp, which is available on BioRxiv at https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.03.26.645465. Questions about these files can be sent to the corresponding author, Patricia J. Wittkopp, at wittkopp@umich.edu.

For additional information, see the included "Zenodo README.pdf" file.

metadata.title: Liriomyza sativae Blanchard
metadata.description:
Liriomyza sativae Blanchard

Material examined. OKLAHOMA: Major Co., Gloss Mountain State Park, 2.v.2021, em. 17.v.2021, E. LoPresti, ex Astragalus missouriensis, # CSE 7009, CNC 1905382 (1♂); TEXAS: Atascosa Co., Pleasanton, Rte. 37 rest stop, 29.033435, −98.431806, 31.iii.2021,em. 22–24.iv.2021, E. LoPresti, ex Lupinus texensis, # CSE 6869, CNC 1905231– 5 (2♂ 3♀); Brooks Co., US-281 rest area, 27.094135, −98.146483, 29–30.iii.2021, em. 14.iv.2021, E. LoPresti, ex Medicago polymorpha, # CSE 6849, CNC 1905220 (1♂); same location, 30.iii.2021, em. by 24.iv.2021, E. LoPresti, ex Nuttallanthus texanus, # CSE 6873, CNC 1905219 (1♂); same but em. 25.iv.2021, ex Sphaeralcea lindheimeri, # CSE 6878, CNC 1905218 (1♂); Hemphill Co., Canadian, Gene Howe WMA, 12.v.2021, em. 1–4.vi.2021, E. LoPresti, ex Astragalus mollissimus, # CSE 7042, CNC 1905327, CNC 1905327 (2♂).

Hosts. (Note: underlined hosts are from a greenhouse experiment.) Amaranthaceae: Amaranthus retroflexus L., Chenopodium album L., Spinacia oleracea L.; Apiaceae: Apium graveolens L., Araliaceae: Hydrocotyle umbellata L.; Asteraceae: Ambrosia acanthicarpa Hook., A.psilostachya DC., “ Aster ”, Bidens L. (“Spanish needle”); Calendula officinalis L., Coreopsis L., Dahlia Cav., Dicoria canescens A.Gray, Felicia bergeriana (Spreng.) O.Hoffm. ex Zahlbr., Galinsoga quadriradiata Ruiz & Pav., Helianthus annuus L., Leucanthemum vulgare Lam., Pericallis × hybrida (Bosse) B.Nord., Rafinesquia neomexicana A.Gray, Senecio vulgaris L., Silybum marianum (L.) Gaertn., Sonchus oleraceus L., Tagetes L., Verbesina helianthoides Michx., V. virginica L., Zinnia L.; Bignoniaceae: Jacaranda Juss.; Brassicaceae: Brassica oleracea L., B. rapa L., “ Nasturtium spp. ”, Raphanus raphanistrum ssp. sativus (L.) Domin; Cucurbitaceae: Citrullus lanatus (Thunb.) Matsum. & Nakai (Stegmaier 1981), Cucumis melo L., C. sativus L., Cucurbita maxima Duchesne, C. pepo L.; Datiscaceae: Datisca glomerata (C.Presl) Baill., Euphorbiaceae: Ricinus communis L.; Fabaceae: Astragalus cicer L., A. *missouriensis Nutt., A. *mollissimus Torr., Bauhinia Plum. ex L., Cajanus cajan (L.) Huth, Lathyrus oleraceus Lam., Lupinus arizonicus (S.Watson) S.Watson, L. *texensis Hook., Medicago lupulina L., M. *polymorpha L., M. sativa L., Melilotus albus Medik. (Stegmaier 1981), M. indicus (L.) All., M. officinalis (L.) Lam., Phaseolus lunatus L., P. vulgaris L. (“greenbeans”), Senna occidentalis (L.) Link, S. tora (L.) Roxb., Trifolium fragiferum L., T. hybridum L., T. incarnatum L., T. pratense L., T. repens L., Vicia nigricans var. gigantea (Hook.) Broich, Vigna luteola (Jacq.) Benth., V. unguiculata (L.) Walp.; Lamiaceae: Melissa officinalis L., Stachys bullata Benth.; Malvaceae: Abelmoschus esculentus (L.) Moench, Alcea rosea L., Anoda cristata (L.) Schltdl., Gossypium hirsutum L., Malva pusilla Sm., Sida acuta Burm.f., * Sphaeralcea lindheimeri A.Gray; Passifloraceae: Passiflora pallens Poepp. ex Mast., P. pallida L.; Plantaginaceae: Antirrhinum majus L., * Nuttallanthus texanus (Scheele) D.A.Sutton, Plantago L., P. major L.; Poaceae: Hordeum vulgare L.; Polygonaceae: Polygonum aviculare L.; Ranunculaceae: Aquilegia L., Ranunculus L.; Rosaceae: Prunus andersonii A.Gray; Solanaceae: Capsicum L. (Stegmaier 1981), C. annuum L., Cestrum diurnum L., C. nocturnum L., Datura metel L., Petunia Juss., Physalis angulata L., Solanum lycopersicum L., S. melongena L., S. nigrum L., S. tuberosum L.; [Verbenaceae: Verbena L.] (Eiseman & Lonsdale 2018, unless otherwise indicated). Other records not confirmed by rearing are summarized by Eiseman & Lonsdale (2018). Numerous other hosts are recorded outside of North America (Lonsdale 2011).

Leaf mine. (Eiseman & Lonsdale 2018: Fig. 151) Whitish, linear, with frass usually in conspicuous black strips on alternating sides. The mines on Sphaeralcea lindheimeri were more irregular and had frass mostly in beaded strips and discrete particles. Most mines are on the upper leaf surface, but an underside mine was observed on Lupinus texensis.

Puparium. Yellow; formed outside the mine.

Phenology and voltinism. Spencer & Steyskal (1986) noted that this species has “several generations in quick successsion.” In general, the egg stage lasts 2–5 days and larvae last 4–7 days above a mean temperature of 24°C, but the entire life cycle can take from less than three weeks to more than nine weeks (Lonsdale et al. 2023).

Distribution. USA: AL, CA, CO, FL, GA, HI, KS, LA, MD, [NM], OH (greenhouse), *OK, PA (greenhouse), SC, VA, TX, WY; Canada: ON; Neotropical Region; introduced in the Afrotropical, Australian, Oriental and Palaearctic Regions (Eiseman & Lonsdale 2018; Lonsdale 2021; Lonsdale et al. 2023).

metadata.title: The Phantasic Profile of a Language
metadata.description: The phantasic profile of a language community, the statistical distribution of voluntary mental-imagery and multimodal-simulation capacity across its speakers, is a load-bearing variable in what the community's vocabulary means. A word produced and prestiged within a community skewed toward hyperphantasic capacity addresses a thicker multimodal semantic region than the same word would address in a community skewed toward aphantasic capacity. Cross-linguistic semantic analysis that ignores this variable systematically misreads imagistic, somatic, aesthetic, and contemplative vocabulary, by assuming reader-side activation capacity is uniform across language communities when it demonstrably is not. The paper lifts an established finding in cognitive science (individual phantasic variation, Zeman et al. 2015) to the community level, argues that phantasic profile is recoverable through inference from literary corpus, contemplative-tradition evidence, and contemporary cross-linguistic data, and proposes that phantasic-profile inference become a standard component of contested-term translation. Worked examples from Buddhist contemplative vocabulary, Old Norse kennings, Japanese aesthetic terms, Hebrew theological vocabulary, and the converse case of scientific-technical English as deliberately aphantasic-compatible lingua franca develop the apparatus across distinct phantasic regimes.
references: Berlin, Brent and Paul Kay. (1969). Basic Color Terms: Their Universality and Evolution. Berkeley: University of California Press.
Beyer, Stephan. (1973). The Cult of Tārā: Magic and Ritual in Tibet. Berkeley: University of California Press.
